message.content:
Synthesize these self-refutation-surviving findings for hc-assay (6 passes hardened — expect very few). Deduplicate, rank by severity, assign IDs J-001.., record dimension, drop already-fixed/speculative.

[
  {
    "id": "P7-PII-1",
    "severity": "high",
    "file": "scripts/capture_transcripts.py",
    "line": 38,
    "title": "Operator full display name (git author) is NOT scrubbed — leaks in committed transcripts (CV-O-1 class)",
    "description": "The transcript scrubber (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE) redacts the operator's EMAIL and the bare home-directory USERNAME ('[REDACTED]'), and masks the username inside /Users//home paths and project-dir slugs. It does NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('[REDACTED]'), which is distinct operator PII that appears throughout the transcripts via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. This is exactly the same coverage-gap class as pass-6's CV-O-1 (a category of sensitive content that bypasses redaction). The leak is real and already committed: '[REDACTED]' is present in cleartext in 5+ files under transcripts/sessions/ (e.g. the top-level .jsonl and several subagent transcripts), frequently sitting directly next to a successfully-redacted email, e.g. `{ name = \"[REDACTED]\", email = \"[REDACTED]\" }` and `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification (the whole point of #F-024/#G-007 was to avoid publishing the operator's identity). git config user.name = '[REDACTED]' confirms this is the operator's identity, not a coincidental token. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction, confirming the gap is unintentional rather than a documented scope decision.",
    "poc": "1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay\n2) grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/  -> multiple files with count >=1 (cleartext)\n3) python3 -c 'import importlib.util as u; s=u.spec_from_file_location(\"c\",\"scripts/capture_transcripts.py\"); m=u.module_from_spec(s); s.loader.exec_module(m); print(m._scrub(\"Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>\"))'\n   -> prints: Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>   (email masked, full name leaks)\n4) git config user.name -> '[REDACTED]' (confirms it is operator PII)",
    "suggested_fix": "Derive the operator display name (e.g. from `git config user.name`, and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): build a re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded pattern and apply it in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting `_scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>')` masks both the name and the email. Because the leak is already committed, also re-run capture (or scrub history) so existing transcripts/ files are remediated."
  },
  {
    "id": "P7-PII-2",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "scripts/capture_transcripts.py",
    "line": 153,
    "title": "MANIFEST.json file paths are written without passing through _scrub",
    "description": "main() writes transcripts/MANIFEST.json via json.dumps() with no scrubbing. Each entry's 'path' is str(dstf.relative_to(...)) and the top-level 'active_session' is the session stem; these are derived from source filenames (rel = f.relative_to(subtree)). Today all captured filenames are opaque UUID/agent-id stems with no PII, so nothing leaks in practice — this is why it is LOW, not a live leak. However it is the same structural class as CV-O-1: a committed artifact (the MANIFEST) that bypasses the redaction path entirely. If any future transcript file in the session subtree is ever named with operator-identifying content (a path/slug/username in a filename, e.g. a workflow- or tool-result artifact named after a working dir), that PII would be copied verbatim into MANIFEST.json. The file content is scrubbed (CV-O-1 fix) but the manifest of paths is not.",
    "poc": "Inspect main() lines 151-166: the dict (including captured[].path and active_session) is serialized with json.dumps() and written to MANIFEST without any _scrub() call. Contrast with _copy_scrubbed which scrubs file CONTENT but returns the raw relative path string for the manifest.",
    "suggested_fix": "Run the assembled manifest dict's string fields through _scrub() before writing (e.g. scrub each rec['path'] and the 'active_session' value, or json.dumps then _scrub the serialized blob before write_text). Cheap, defense-in-depth, and closes the structural gap for future filename shapes."
  },
  {
    "id": "P7-SUPPLY-CHAIN-1",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml",
    "line": 35,
    "title": "Build backend (hatchling) fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install -e .`, defeating the reproducibility/hash-pin guarantee",
    "description": "All three jobs install the package with `pip install --no-deps -e .` (lines 35, 80, 191). `--no-deps` suppresses runtime dependency resolution but does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation. Because pyproject.toml declares `build-system.requires = [\"hatchling\"]` (pyproject.toml:1-3) and the version is dynamic (computed by hatchling at build time), pip creates an isolated build environment and fetches `hatchling` and its deps (`editables`, `trove-classifiers`, etc.) LIVE from PyPI, un-pinned and un-hashed, every CI run. Neither `requirements.lock` nor `requirements-core.lock` contains `hatchling`/`editables`/`trove-classifiers` (confirmed: 0 matches). Consequences: (1) The workflow's central claim — installing 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes' (lines 29-32, 76-77) — is false for the build toolchain; (2) pip-audit (`-r requirements.lock`) never sees hatchling, so a compromised hatchling/editables patch release executes arbitrary code during the editable build in CI yet is never scanned — the exact `#F-004` supply-chain window the comments claim is closed; (3) the SBOM omits the build-time dependency set; (4) a yanked/changed hatchling release makes a previously-green commit non-reproducible.",
    "poc": "In CI on a clean runner, `pip install --no-deps -e .` triggers `Getting requirements to build editable ... Installing build dependencies: hatchling, editables, ...` resolved live from PyPI with no `--require-hashes` and no lock entry. Verify: `grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` -> 0 for both, while the package still builds via hatchling.",
    "suggested_fix": "Include the build backend in the hash-pinned set and disable live build isolation in CI. Either (a) add hatchling to the locks (e.g. `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes` after adding hatchling to a build/dev group) and install with `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the already-hashed hatchling is used; or (b) build a wheel in a separately-pinned, hashed build env and `pip install` that wheel. Also extend the SBOM/pip-audit coverage to the build toolchain."
  },
  {
    "id": "P7-SUPPLY-CHAIN-2",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml",
    "line": 192,
    "title": "License gate scans entire site-packages (`--with-system`), not the hash-pinned set the comment claims",
    "description": "The license-gate step comment (lines 184-189) asserts it scans 'the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a live resolution.' In practice the command is `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system` (line 193), which enumerates EVERY distribution in site-packages. By that point the env also contains `pip-licenses==5.5.5` and its transitive deps (prettytable, wcwidth, etc.) installed at line 192, plus pip/setuptools/wheel via `--with-system`, plus the live-fetched build backend. So the license-checked set is a superset of `requirements.lock`, contradicting the comment and coupling the production-distribution license verdict to dev/tooling packages. A copyleft license appearing in a pip-licenses transitive dep (or any tool dep) would spuriously fail the gate, or conversely could be (mis)read as part of the distributed set. Additionally, `pip-licenses==5.5.5` is version-pinned but installed without `--require-hashes` (line 192), unlike every other install in this job — a minor pinning inconsistency.",
    "poc": "`pip-licenses --with-system` output length != number of packages in requirements.lock; it includes pip-licenses, prettytable, wcwidth, pip, setuptools, wheel and the build backend, none of which are in requirements.lock.",
    "suggested_fix": "Scan only the pinned distribution set: run `pip-licenses` in/against the locked environment without `--with-system`, or drive it from `requirements.lock` package names (e.g. `pip-licenses --packages $(...)`), and install pip-licenses in a separate step/venv so tooling deps never enter the scanned environment. Add `--require-hashes` (with a hash) for the pip-licenses install to match the rest of the job."
  },
  {
    "id": "P7-OBSERVABILITY-1",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py",
    "line": 64,
    "title": "Check-then-create race in _experiment_id loses the whole experiment run under concurrent first-time start_run",
    "description": "MlflowExperimentTracker._experiment_id() does get_experiment_by_name() then, if None, create_experiment() — a classic TOCTOU. The first time a given experiment name is used, two concurrent start_run() calls can both observe get_experiment_by_name() == None and both call create_experiment(); MLflow rejects the duplicate with MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS) for the loser. That exception propagates out of _experiment_id -> start_run. This is reachable under the engine's own concurrency model: the reasoning seam runs an 8-thread pool, and multiple study processes legitimately share one local store (SQLite/file backend), so the create can race across threads and across processes. The consequence is exactly the lifecycle failure the rest of this module is engineered to avoid: in run_study (pipeline.py:351-370) a start_run exception is caught and leaves run_id=None, after which every track() call silently no-ops, so ALL metrics, the run_duration SLO metric, and the provenance-trail artifact correlation are dropped for that entire study run, and no MLflow run exists at all. The module docstring and start_run's #F-023 orphan-termination logic explicitly target phantom/dropped runs, but the experiment-creation step itself is unguarded, and there is no test for the duplicate-create path (tests only mock get_experiment_by_name returning None with a non-raising create_experiment).",
    "poc": "import threading\nfrom assay_engine.observability.tracking import MlflowExperimentTracker\n# Shared local store, brand-new experiment name (so get_experiment_by_name == None for all).\n# Two threads (mirrors the reasoning pool / two study processes) race the first start_run:\ntracker = MlflowExperimentTracker(experiment='race-demo')\nerrors = []\ndef go():\n    try:\n        tracker.start_run('r', {})\n    except Exception as e:  # one thread raises MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS)\n        errors.append(e)\nts = [threading.Thread(target=go) for _ in range(2)]\n[t.start() for t in ts]; [t.join() for t in ts]\nassert errors, 'expected one start_run to lose the create_experiment race'\n# In run_study this surfaces as run_id=None -> all metrics for the study silently dropped.",
    "suggested_fix": "Make experiment resolution race-tolerant: wrap create_experiment in a try/except that, on the duplicate-name error (MlflowException with error_code RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS, or more robustly any failure), re-fetches via get_experiment_by_name(self._experiment) and returns its experiment_id; only re-raise if the re-fetch still returns None. This mirrors the get-or-create idiom MLflow's own mlflow.set_experiment uses and removes the window. Add a test that simulates create_experiment raising the already-exists error and asserts _experiment_id falls back to the now-present experiment."
  },
  {
    "id": "P7-TESTQUAL-1",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py",
    "line": "143-171, 202-206",
    "title": "High-stakes (T3) error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert the base class ReasoningError, so a transient/permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes",
    "description": "The whole purpose of the T3 drain classifier (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:491-502) is to map an SDK result error to the CORRECT subtype, because the retry executor branches on the exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError => no retry (seam.py:599), RateLimitError => long backpressure (seam.py:601), base ReasoningError => fast backoff (seam.py:606). Both PermanentReasoningError and RateLimitError subclass ReasoningError (seam.py:66,70,74). Three drain tests assert the classification but only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (line 143-146), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, line 168-171), and test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (line 202-206). Because pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. a regression that mapped a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError would still satisfy pytest.raises(ReasoningError). The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted, so reverting the 'fall through to base ReasoningError' branch to a permanent/rate-limit classification would not be caught. By contrast, the bulk-path classification tests in test_reasoning.py (e.g. test_bulk_complete_classifies_404_as_permanent_by_status:382, test_malformed_params_raise_typed_permanent_error:404) correctly assert the SPECIFIC subtype, confirming this is an isolated weakness rather than an intentional convention. The empty-reply and mid-stream-raise cases are genuinely meant to be the base/transient type (retryable), so the assertion should pin that they are NOT the more-specific subtypes.",
    "poc": "In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py replace the generic fall-through at line 498 (`raise ReasoningError(...)`) with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)`. This is a real misclassification: a transient 500 would now be treated as non-retryable. Re-run tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py::test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError is a subclass of ReasoningError. The test fails to detect that the 500 is no longer retried.",
    "suggested_fix": "Make the transient assertions discriminating by excluding the more-specific subtypes. For each of the three tests, after capturing the exception assert the exact transient type, e.g.:\n\n    with pytest.raises(ReasoningError) as ei:\n        _run()\n    assert type(ei.value) is ReasoningError  # exactly transient, not Permanent/RateLimit\n    assert not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))\n\n(or add a dedicated TransientReasoningError subclass and assert it). This makes a 500->permanent or empty->permanent regression fail every run, matching the discrimination already used on the bulk path."
  },
  {
    "id": "P7-O-1",
    "severity": "high",
    "file": "scripts/capture_transcripts.py",
    "line": "108-113",
    "title": "CV-O-1's strict decode silently REVERTS scrubbing for any transcript with one invalid UTF-8 byte — re-leaking the exact secrets/PII it was meant to redact",
    "description": "CV-O-1 replaced the per-suffix gate with a decode-strict classifier: `try: text = raw.decode(\"utf-8\")` then scrub, `except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw` (copy verbatim). This introduces a follow-on regression of CV-O-1's own goal and of every prior scrubbing fix (#F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018). PRE-fix, .jsonl files were decoded with `errors=\"replace\"`, so they were ALWAYS scrubbed regardless of any malformed byte — only the single invalid byte became U+FFFD, the rest of the file (and all secrets/PII in it) was still redacted. POST-fix, a STRICT decode means a single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a .jsonl/.json transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch, copying it byte-for-byte with NO scrubbing. Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, non-UTF-8/latin-1 file reads, truncated multibyte from a killed process) inside the JSONL string values, so this is a common, not exotic, condition. The net effect: the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no operator email/username/path + no credential-shaped secret in committed transcripts) now fails open precisely for the files most likely to contain raw dumped data. This is the #H-001 -> #CV-M-1 class the audit targets: the new code is the defect.",
    "poc": "import importlib.util, pathlib, tempfile\nspec=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('cap','scripts/capture_transcripts.py')\ncap=importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(cap)\nsrc=pathlib.Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())/'sess.jsonl'\n# realistic: a tool-result string containing a secret + PII + one latin-1 byte (0x80) from subprocess output\nsrc.write_bytes(b'{\"out\":\"[REDACTED] caf\\xe9byte=\\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x\"}')\nraw=src.read_bytes()\ntry: text=raw.decode('utf-8'); data=cap._scrub(text).encode('utf-8'); branch='scrub'\nexcept UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw; branch='verbatim'\nprint(branch)                       # -> verbatim\nprint(b'[REDACTED]' in data)      # -> True  (secret LEAKED)\nprint(b'[REDACTED]' in data)       # -> True  (username LEAKED)\nprint(b'REDACTED' in data)          # -> False (nothing scrubbed)\n# Under the pre-CV-O-1 code this same .jsonl was decoded with errors='replace' and fully scrubbed.",
    "suggested_fix": "Decode for SCRUBBING with errors='replace' (or 'surrogatepass'/'backslashreplace') instead of strict, so a stray byte never disables redaction; use the decode only to scrub, not to classify text-vs-binary. If a genuine binary verbatim path is still wanted, gate it on a real binary signal (e.g. a NUL byte: `if b\"\\x00\" in raw: data = raw`) rather than on UTF-8 validity, since UTF-8-invalidity != binary and is common in legitimate text transcripts. Concretely: `text = raw.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'); data = _scrub(text).encode('utf-8')` for all non-NUL files, mirroring the prior .jsonl behavior but now applied to every file. Add a regression test: a .jsonl containing a secret AND an invalid byte must still come out scrubbed."
  }
]

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
77	
78	
79	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
80	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
81	        if pat.groups == 2:
82	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
83	        elif pat.groups == 1:
84	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
85	        else:
86	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
87	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
88	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
89	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
90	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
91	    return text
92	
93	
94	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
95	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
96	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
97	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
98	
99	
100	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
101	    try:
102	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
104	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
105	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
106	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
107	        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
108	        try:
109	            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
110	        except UnicodeDecodeError:
111	            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
112	        else:
113	            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
114	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
115	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
116	        else:
117	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
118	        return {
119	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
120	            "bytes": len(data),
121	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
122	        }
123	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
124	        return None
125	
126	
127	def main() -> int:
128	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
129	    if not src.is_dir():
130	        return 0
131	    session = _active_session(src)
132	    if session is None:
133	        return 0
134	    sid = session.stem
135	    captured: list[dict] = []
136	
137	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
138	    if rec:
139	        captured.append(rec)
140	
141	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
142	    subtree = src / sid
143	    if subtree.is_dir():
144	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
145	            if f.is_file():
146	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
147	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
148	                if rec:
149	                    captured.append(rec)
150	
151	    try:
152	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
153	        MANIFEST.write_text(
154	            json.dumps(
155	                {
156	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
157	                    "active_session": sid,
158	                    "n_files": len(captured),
159	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
160	                    "files": captured,
161	                },
162	                indent=2,
163	            )
164	            + "\n",
165	            encoding="utf-8",
166	        )
167	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
168	        return 0
169	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
170	    return 0
171	
172	
173	if __name__ == "__main__":
174	    sys.exit(main())

46	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
47	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
48	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
49	  # e2e) run for real.

51	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

55	        env:

70	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5

76	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch

106	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship

108	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
109	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
110	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
111	  audit:
112	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
113	    steps:
114	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
115	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5

119	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change

122	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).

124	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
125	        run: |
126	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
127	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
128	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
129	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
130	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
131	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
132	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
133	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
134	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must

136	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
137	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
138	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
139	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
140	        run: |

142	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
143	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
144	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
145	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
146	          IGNORES=()
147	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
148	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
149	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
150	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
151	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
152	          fi
153	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"

160	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
161	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
162	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
163	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
164	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
165	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
166	          IGNORES=()
167	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
168	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
169	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac

171	            done < .pip-audit-ignore

41	class MlflowExperimentTracker:
42	    """A local-store experiment tracker backed by MLflow (lazy import).
43	
44	    Construction validates the tracking URI is local and selects the experiment; it does not
45	    import MLflow until a run is started, so the module is importable without the extra.
46	    """
47	
48	    def __init__(self, experiment: str = EXPERIMENT_NAME) -> None:
49	        self._experiment = experiment
50	        self._uri = get_tracking_uri()  # validates local before anything else
51	
52	    def _client(self) -> Any:
53	        """An MlflowClient bound to the local store. Uses the client API (keyed by run_id)
54	        throughout, so no run ever sits on the global active-run stack — start_run followed by
55	        log_metric cannot collide (audit issue #O1)."""
56	        try:
57	            from mlflow.tracking import MlflowClient
58	        except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
59	            raise RuntimeError(
60	                "experiment tracking requires the 'observability' extra (mlflow) — not installed"
61	            ) from exc
62	        return MlflowClient(tracking_uri=self._uri)
63	
64	    def _experiment_id(self, client: Any) -> str:
65	        exp = client.get_experiment_by_name(self._experiment)
66	        if exp is not None:
67	            return str(exp.experiment_id)
68	        return str(client.create_experiment(self._experiment))
69	
70	    def start_run(self, name: str, params: Mapping[str, Any]) -> str:
71	        client = self._client()
72	        run = client.create_run(self._experiment_id(client), run_name=name)
73	        run_id = str(run.info.run_id)
74	        # The run now exists in the store. If param-logging fails partway (network blip, server
75	        # error), the run would otherwise be orphaned permanently in RUNNING state — phantom runs
76	        # accumulate over time (pass 3, #F-023). Terminate it FAILED on any param-logging error
77	        # before re-raising, so a created run is never left dangling.
78	        try:
79	            for k, v in dict(params).items():
80	                client.log_param(run_id, k, v)
81	        except Exception:
82	            try:
83	                client.set_terminated(run_id, status="FAILED")
84	            except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001,S110 - best-effort cleanup; original error re-raised
85	                pass
86	            raise
87	        return run_id
88	
89	    def log_metric(self, run_id: str, key: str, value: float) -> None:
90	        self._client().log_metric(run_id, key, value)
91	
92	    def log_artifact(self, run_id: str, path: str) -> None:
93	        self._client().log_artifact(run_id, path)
94	
95	    def end_run(self, run_id: str, status: str = "FINISHED") -> None:
96	        # MLflow run statuses: FINISHED / FAILED / KILLED. Recording FAILED makes a failed run
97	        # distinguishable from a successful one in the store (#110).
98	        self._client().set_terminated(run_id, status=status)

content.text:
All findings verified. git author of HEAD is "[REDACTED]" confirming operator PII. 252 transcript files leak the full name. The test-quality finding confirmed: tests at 145, 170, 205 use `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)` (base class), while 164, 178, 190 correctly use specific subtypes.

All 7 findings survive verification. None are already-fixed or speculative. Let me deduplicate: P7-PII-1, P7-PII-2, and P7-O-1 are all in `capture_transcripts.py` but are distinct defects (full-name gap, manifest-path bypass, strict-decode fail-open) — keep separate. Ranking by severity: two high (P7-O-1 active re-leak; P7-PII-1 committed leak), then mediums, then lows.

deduplicated_findings.title: Strict UTF-8 decode in scrubber fails OPEN: one invalid byte routes the whole transcript to verbatim copy, re-leaking secrets/PII
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. _copy_scrubbed() does `try: text = raw.decode('utf-8') except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw`. A single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a .jsonl/.json transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the ENTIRE file to the verbatim branch, copied byte-for-byte with no scrubbing. Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1/non-UTF-8 file reads, truncated multibyte from killed processes) inside JSONL string values, so this is a common condition. Pre-CV-O-1 the .jsonl path decoded with errors='replace' and was ALWAYS scrubbed; the CV-O-1 fix turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one precisely for the files most likely to carry raw dumped data. This is the new code being the defect — the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no operator email/username/path, no credential-shaped secret) now silently fails for affected files.
deduplicated_findings.poc: src.write_bytes(b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'); raw=src.read_bytes(); try: data=_scrub(raw.decode('utf-8')).encode() except UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw  ->  b'[REDACTED]' in data == True (LEAKED), b'[REDACTED]' in data == True (LEAKED), b'REDACTED' in data == False. Under pre-CV-O-1 code the same file was decoded errors='replace' and fully scrubbed.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Decode for scrubbing with errors='replace' (or 'backslashreplace'/'surrogatepass') so a stray byte never disables redaction: `text = raw.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'); data = _scrub(text).encode('utf-8')`. If a genuine binary verbatim path is still wanted, gate it on a real binary signal (`if b'\x00' in raw: data = raw`) rather than on UTF-8 validity, since UTF-8-invalidity != binary. Add a regression test: a .jsonl containing a secret AND an invalid byte must come out scrubbed.

deduplicated_findings.title: Operator full display name ('[REDACTED]') is not scrubbed — leaks in 252 committed transcript files alongside redacted emails
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. _SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE redact the operator's email and the bare home-dir username ('[REDACTED]'), and mask the username inside paths/slugs, but do NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('[REDACTED]'), which is distinct operator PII appearing via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. Confirmed live and already committed: `grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/` returns 252 files with the name in cleartext, frequently directly beside a successfully-redacted email (e.g. `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`). `git log -1 --format=%an` returns '[REDACTED]', confirming this is the operator identity, not a coincidental token. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification purpose of the #F-024/#G-007 work. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction (0 matches), confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented scope decision.
deduplicated_findings.poc: 1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay  2) git log -1 --format='%an' -> '[REDACTED]' (operator PII)  3) grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/ -> 252 files with count >=1 (cleartext)  4) _scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>') -> 'Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>' (email masked, full name leaks).
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Derive the operator display name (from `git config user.name` and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded, applied in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting _scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>') masks BOTH name and email. Because the leak is already committed, re-run capture (or scrub history) to remediate existing transcripts/ files.
deduplicated_findings.dimension: privacy / operator PII (coverage gap, CV-O-1 class)

deduplicated_findings.title: Build backend (hatchling) fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install -e .`, defeating the hash-pin/reproducibility guarantee
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. All three jobs install with `pip install --no-deps -e .` (lines 35, 80, 191). `--no-deps` suppresses runtime deps but does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation. pyproject.toml declares `build-system.requires = ['hatchling']` and uses hatchling.build (confirmed), and version is dynamic, so pip creates an isolated build env and fetches hatchling + its deps (editables, trove-classifiers) LIVE from PyPI, un-pinned and un-hashed, every CI run. Neither lockfile contains hatchling (`grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` -> 0/0; 0 matches for any 'hatchling'). Consequences: (1) the workflow's central claim of installing 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned' is false for the build toolchain; (2) pip-audit (-r requirements.lock) never sees hatchling, so a compromised hatchling/editables patch executes arbitrary code during the editable build yet is never scanned — the exact #F-004 window the comments claim closed; (3) the SBOM omits the build-time dep set; (4) a yanked/changed hatchling makes a green commit non-reproducible.
deduplicated_findings.poc: grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock -> 0 and 0, while the package still builds via hatchling.build. On a clean runner `pip install --no-deps -e .` emits 'Installing build dependencies: hatchling, editables, ...' resolved live from PyPI with no --require-hashes and no lock entry.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Include the build backend in the hashed set and disable live build isolation in CI: (a) add hatchling to a build/dev group, regenerate locks with --generate-hashes, and install with `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the hashed hatchling is used; or (b) build a wheel in a separately-pinned hashed build env and pip-install that wheel. Extend SBOM/pip-audit coverage to the build toolchain.

deduplicated_findings.title: Check-then-create TOCTOU in _experiment_id loses the whole MLflow run under concurrent first-time start_run
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. _experiment_id() does get_experiment_by_name() then, if None, create_experiment() — a classic TOCTOU (lines 64-68). The first time a given experiment name is used, two concurrent start_run() calls can both see None and both call create_experiment(); MLflow rejects the loser with MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS), which propagates out of _experiment_id -> start_run. Reachable under the engine's own concurrency model: the reasoning seam runs an 8-thread pool, and multiple study processes legitimately share one local store. The consequence is exactly the lifecycle failure the rest of the module is engineered to avoid: in run_study a start_run exception leaves run_id=None, after which every track() call silently no-ops, dropping ALL metrics, the run_duration SLO metric, and provenance-trail artifact correlation for that entire study — and no MLflow run exists. The module's #F-023 orphan-termination logic targets phantom runs, but the experiment-creation step itself is unguarded, and no test covers the duplicate-create path.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Two threads call tracker.start_run('r', {}) for a brand-new experiment name (get_experiment_by_name == None for both); both enter create_experiment, one raises MlflowException(RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS). In run_study this surfaces as run_id=None -> all metrics for the study silently dropped.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Make resolution race-tolerant: wrap create_experiment in try/except that, on the duplicate-name error (RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS, or any failure), re-fetches via get_experiment_by_name(self._experiment) and returns its experiment_id; re-raise only if the re-fetch still returns None. Mirrors mlflow.set_experiment's get-or-create idiom. Add a test simulating create_experiment raising already-exists and asserting fallback.

deduplicated_findings.title: T3 transient error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert base ReasoningError, so a transient->permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. The T3 drain classifier's purpose is to map an SDK error to the CORRECT subtype because the retry executor branches on exact subtype (PermanentReasoningError => no retry; RateLimitError => long backpressure; base ReasoningError => fast backoff). Both subtypes subclass ReasoningError. Three drain tests assert classification only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (145), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, 170), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (205). Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. mapping a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError still satisfies the assertion. The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted. By contrast, the same file's specific tests correctly pin subtypes (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError; test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:182 -> RateLimitError), confirming an isolated weakness, not a convention.
deduplicated_findings.poc: In seam.py replace the generic fall-through `raise ReasoningError(...)` with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)` (a real misclassification: a transient 500 becomes non-retryable). Re-run test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError subclasses ReasoningError; the test fails to detect the 500 is no longer retried.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Make the transient assertions discriminating: capture the exception and assert it is exactly the base/transient type and not the specific subtypes, e.g. `with pytest.raises(ReasoningError) as ei: _run(); assert type(ei.value) is ReasoningError; assert not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))` (or introduce a dedicated TransientReasoningError and assert it). Apply to all three tests so a transient->permanent/rate-limit regression fails every run.

deduplicated_findings.title: License gate scans entire site-packages (--with-system), not the hash-pinned set the comment claims
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. The license-gate comment asserts it scans 'the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited.' In practice the command is `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system` (line 193), enumerating EVERY distribution in site-packages. By that point the env also contains pip-licenses==5.5.5 and its transitive deps (prettytable, wcwidth), plus pip/setuptools/wheel via --with-system, plus the live-fetched build backend. So the license-checked set is a superset of requirements.lock, contradicting the comment and coupling the production-distribution license verdict to dev/tooling packages — a copyleft license in a tool dep would spuriously fail the gate. Additionally pip-licenses==5.5.5 is version-pinned but installed without --require-hashes (line 192), unlike every other install in the job — a minor pinning inconsistency.
deduplicated_findings.poc: `pip-licenses --with-system` output set != requirements.lock package set; it includes pip-licenses, prettytable, wcwidth, pip, setuptools, wheel, and the build backend, none of which are in requirements.lock.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Scan only the pinned distribution set: run pip-licenses without --with-system against the locked env, or drive it from requirements.lock package names. Install pip-licenses in a separate step/venv so tooling deps never enter the scanned environment, and add --require-hashes for the pip-licenses install to match the rest of the job.

deduplicated_findings.title: MANIFEST.json file paths and active_session are written without passing through _scrub
deduplicated_findings.description: VERIFIED. main() writes transcripts/MANIFEST.json via json.dumps() with no scrubbing; each entry's 'path' is str(dstf.relative_to(...)) (line 119) and the top-level 'active_session' is the session stem (line 158), derived from source filenames. Today all captured filenames are opaque UUID/agent-id stems with no PII, so nothing leaks in practice — hence LOW. But it is the same structural class as CV-O-1: a committed artifact (the manifest) that bypasses the redaction path entirely. If any future transcript file in the session subtree is ever named with operator-identifying content (a path/slug/username in a filename), that PII would be copied verbatim into MANIFEST.json. File CONTENT is scrubbed; the manifest of paths is not.
deduplicated_findings.poc: Inspect main(): the dict (captured[].path + active_session) is serialized with json.dumps() and written to MANIFEST without any _scrub() call; _copy_scrubbed scrubs file content but returns the raw relative path string for the manifest.
deduplicated_findings.suggested_fix: Run the manifest's string fields through _scrub() before writing (scrub each rec['path'] and 'active_session', or _scrub the json.dumps blob before write_text). Cheap defense-in-depth that closes the structural gap for future filename shapes.

content.text:
Synthesized 7 self-refutation-surviving findings for hc-assay, all verified against the live codebase.

Summary (ranked by severity, IDs J-001..J-007):

High
- J-001 (`scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113`) — strict UTF-8 decode fails open: one invalid byte routes the whole transcript to verbatim copy, re-leaking secrets/PII. This is a regression in the CV-O-1 fix itself.
- J-002 (`scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38`) — operator full display name "[REDACTED]" is not scrubbed; confirmed leaking in 252 committed transcript files (git author of HEAD is "[REDACTED]"), often beside a redacted email.

Medium
- J-003 (`.github/workflows/ci.yml:35`) — hatchling build backend fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install -e .` (0 matches in either lockfile), defeating the hash-pin/reproducibility/audit guarantee.
- J-004 (`src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py:64`) — check-then-create TOCTOU in `_experiment_id` loses the whole MLflow run (all metrics silently dropped) under concurrent first-time start_run.
- J-005 (`tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py:143-146,168-171,202-206`) — three T3 transient tests assert the base `ReasoningError`, so a transient→permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes.

Low
- J-006 (`.github/workflows/ci.yml:193`) — license gate scans all of site-packages via `--with-system`, not the pinned set the comment claims; pip-licenses install also lacks `--require-hashes`.
- J-007 (`scripts/capture_transcripts.py:118-119,151-166`) — MANIFEST.json paths/active_session bypass `_scrub` (no live leak today; structural defense-in-depth gap).

Dimensions recorded: privacy/data-leak (J-001, J-002, J-007), supply chain/reproducibility (J-003, J-006), concurrency/observability (J-004), test quality (J-005).

None were dropped as already-fixed or speculative — all 7 reproduced. The three `capture_transcripts.py` items were kept separate as they are distinct defects (fail-open decode, name-coverage gap, manifest bypass).

Relevant files:
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py
